questions.text: When Babur receives news of subordinates who have demonstrated loyalty through difficult circumstances, how does he typically respond in terms of material rewards?
questions.held_out_passage: Not to make vain the hope with which some 7 or 8,000 _Nūḥānī_ Afghāns had come in to me, I bestowed 50 _laks_ from Bīhār on Maḥmūd Khān _Nūḥānī_, after reserving one _krūr_ for Government uses (_khalṣa_), and gave the remainder of the Bihār revenues in trust for the above-mentioned Jalāl Khān who for his part agreed to pay one _krūr_ of tribute.

questions.text: When military objectives have been substantially achieved and environmental conditions become unfavorable, what decision does Babur make regarding continued campaigning?
questions.held_out_passage: As the object of this campaign was to put down the rebel Afghāns of whom some had taken their heads and gone off, some had come in submissive and accepting my service, and the remaining few were in the hands of the Bengalī (Naṣrat Shāh) who had taken them in charge, and as, moreover, the Rains were near, we in our turn wrote and despatched words for peace on the conditions mentioned.

questions.text: How does Babur handle situations where subordinates have been forced into difficult political positions due to external pressures beyond their control?

questions.text: Who translated the Bābur-Nāma from the original Turki text into English?
In what year was the English translation of the Bābur-Nāma first printed?
What was Bābur's father's name?
Name two of Bābur's uncles mentioned in the text.
Which city did Bābur successfully capture in 903 AH (1497-1498 AD)?
What was the approximate size of Bābur's force when he was left with very few men after losing Samarkand and Andijān?
To which city did Bābur's mother's uncle `Alī-dost invite him?
What city did Bābur recover after the Mughūls were defeated near Akhsī?
Who was Bābur's half-brother for whom the Mughūls demanded and besieged Andijān?
In what language was the original Bābur-Nāma written?
Why would Babur's loss of both Samarkand and Andijan simultaneously have been particularly devastating to his position, given what the text reveals about his family situation and available resources?
What does the pattern of Babur being forced to retreat to Khujand multiple times suggest about the relationship between his ambitions and his actual military capacity during this period?
How did the presence of Babur's mother, grandmother, and his followers' families in Khujand complicate his military strategy, based on the events described in the text?
What can be inferred about The Khan's reliability as an ally from the multiple instances where he offered help but then withdrew or was persuaded to retire?
Why would Babur's decision to leave Samarkand in his cousin Ali's hands have been strategically risky, given the subsequent events involving the Mughul faction?
How did the internal conflicts among the Timurid Mirzas and between Khusrau Shah and Mas'ud Miranshahi create both opportunities and obstacles for Babur's territorial ambitions?
What does the text suggest about why Babur was able to recover Andijan after initially losing it, and what does this reveal about the stability of his enemies' positions?
How did Babur's illness episodes, mentioned multiple times in the text, appear to affect both his military campaigns and his political standing with his supporters?
Based on the sequence of events involving Ahmad Tambal and the Mughul faction, what can be inferred about the nature of the threat they posed compared to other rivals?
Why would Babur's need to borrow Pashaghar for winter and later move to summer-pastures indicate about the precariousness of his territorial control during this period?
What does the repeated pattern of Babur's force being 'insufficient' for his objectives suggest about the gap between his ambitions and the resources available to him?
What would Babur's opinion be on the use of gunpowder artillery in 21st-century warfare?
How would Babur have responded to the invention of the steam engine and industrial manufacturing?
What were Babur's private thoughts on the theological debates within Sufism that occurred after his death?
If Babur had access to modern genetic testing, what would he have discovered about his ancestry beyond what he recorded in the Baburnama?
How would Babur have adapted his military strategies if he had possessed knowledge of modern satellite reconnaissance?
What did Babur believe about the scientific theories of heliocentrism and gravity?
How would Babur have governed the Mughal Empire differently if he had lived to age 100?
What was Babur's stance on the philosophical implications of the printing press technology that emerged after his lifetime?
If Babur could have witnessed the partition of India in 1947, what would his reaction have been?
What personal correspondence did Babur exchange with European monarchs regarding trade and diplomacy?

Babur repeatedly lost and regained territories (Samarkand, Andijan) through military campaigns, yet also showed willingness to negotiate and make accommodations. At what point would he have chosen permanent retreat over continued conflict, and what would that threshold have been?

The text notes Babur's 'adoption of orthodox observance' during his early reign. How did this religious commitment interact with his pragmatic military alliances with groups of varying religious strictness, and did he ever face genuine moral conflict between these positions?

Babur was distressed to tears when separated from his family and supporters, yet he repeatedly made strategic decisions that prolonged their separation. How did he rationalize emotional vulnerability against the demands of ambition, and did this balance shift over time?

Given that Babur cursed Khusrau Shah for blinding his cousin Masud, what was the actual limit of his willingness to forgive rivals, and would he have extended mercy to someone who had committed similar atrocities against his own family?

Babur explicitly spoke of his 'ambition to rule,' yet he also depended heavily on his uncle The Khan's military support. At what point would his ambition have required him to move against his own family member, and would he have been capable of doing so?

The text shows Babur borrowing territory (Pashaghar) and being 'admitted to Marghiyan on terms,' suggesting he accepted subordinate positions temporarily. What was his actual tolerance for dependency, and when would accepting help have felt like unacceptable loss of autonomy?

Babur had a relapse of illness where he was 'believed dying,' yet recovered to continue campaigning. How did near-death experiences affect his decision-making calculus, and did they make him more or less willing to take risks with his remaining forces?

Multiple followers abandoned Babur to return to their families in Andijan. How did Babur genuinely view the loyalty-versus-family-obligation conflict his men faced, and would he have punished those who left, or did he accept this as inevitable?

Babur left Samarkand in his cousin Ali's hands, but then lost it. Did Babur blame Ali, himself, or circumstance, and how did this failure affect his subsequent trust in delegating authority to family members?

The text describes Babur being 'unwilling to burden' Khujand longer and moving to summer pastures. Was this genuine consideration for his hosts, strategic necessity, or a rationalization for his inability to hold territory—and how would Babur himself have characterized his motivation?
